coredata.dc:title: Pt nanoclusters based on N, S co-doped graphene quantum dots as a smart probe for ultrasensitive Ag+ sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Xue, Ziyu
Bai, Dehong
Li, Yu
Zhang, Qiankun
shao, Tao
Guo, Peiqing
Zhang, Dongxia
Zhou, Xibin
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a simple strategy for the rapid detection of Ag+ based on nitrogen, sulfur doped graphene quantum dots (N, S-GQDs) decorated platinum nanoclusters (PtNCs) was proposed for the first time. N, S-GQDs decorated PtNCs (N, S-GQDs@PtNCs) were prepared by a reduction method using ascorbic acid (AA) as reducing agent and N, S-GQDs as a stabilizer and morphology directing reagent, respectively. The N, S-GQDs@PtNCs have an excellent peroxidase activity, which could catalyze the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) by H2O2 to lead to an obvious color change. However, Ag+ could be reduced to Ag0 by N,S-GQDs, and the Ag0 covered the surface of N, S-GQDs@PtNCs causing its peroxidase activity to decrease. Ag+ concentration could be quantified by measuring the maximum absorption signal at 652 nm for oxidized TMB with a wide linear range of 0.5–300.0 nM and a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 0.2 nM. In addition, satisfactory results were obtained for the detection of Ag+ in actual water samples. The prepared colorimetric sensor had advantages such as simple operation, cost-effective, fast response, and visualization, and had great potential in detecting toxic Ag+ in environmental.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N, S-GQDs
Platinum nanoclusters
Colorimetric sensor
Silver ions detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the formed N, S-GQDs@PtNCs
Effect of Ag+ on the catalytic activity of N, S-GQDs@PtNCs
Sensitivity of the Ag+ sensing system

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science and Technology Support Projects of Gansu Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

Gansu Province Science and Technology Department

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Financial was sponsored by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21165016 , 21175108 , and 21265018 ), the Science and Technology Support Projects of Gansu Province (No.s 1011GKCA025 , 090GKCA036 , 1208RJZM289 ). the Postgraduate Research Funding Projects of Northwest Normal University ( 2021KYZZ02140 ).

Financial was sponsored by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21165016, 21175108, and 21265018), the Science and Technology Support Projects of Gansu Province (No.s 1011GKCA025, 090GKCA036, 1208RJZM289). the Postgraduate Research Funding Projects of Northwest Normal University (2021KYZZ02140).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of N, S-GQDs (A) and N, S-GQDs@PtNCs (B).
XPS spectrum showing the binding energy of Pt 4f.
Steady-state kinetic analyses using the Michaelis–Menten model (A) and Lineweaver–Burk model for N, S-GQDs@PtNCs (B).
XPS spectrum showing the binding energy of Ag 3d.

The effect of experimental conditions on the sensing performance (A) the concentration of N, S-GQDs@PtNCs (B) the concentration of H2O2 (C) the concentration of TMB (D) the reaction time between Ag+ and TMB-H2O2-N, S-GQDs@PtNCs system.

(A) Absorption spectra of TMB solution under different concentrations of Ag+ (a) 0 nM (b) 0.5 nM (c) 10 nM (d) 20 nM (e) 40 nM (f) 60 nM (g) 100 nM (h) 150 nM (i) 200 nM (j) 250 nM (k) 300 nM; (B) The linear relationship between absorption signal and Ag+ concentration.
Selectivity of colorimetric sensing for different metal cations. (The concentration of Ag+ was 300 nM, and other cations are 6 μM, EDTA: 1 mM).
Schematic diagram of the N, S-GQDs@PtNCs based colorimetric visual sensor for Ag+ detection.
Comparison of our present work with other methods for Ag+ detection.
Recovery of standard addition to measure the Ag+ in tap water and Yellow river.

head.ce:title: Pt nanoclusters based on N, S co-doped graphene quantum dots as a smart probe for ultrasensitive Ag+ sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratouy of Resource Environment and Sustainable Development of Oasis, Gansu Province, College of Geography and Environmental Science, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratouy of Resource Environment and Sustainable Development of Oasis, Gansu Province
College of Geography and Environmental Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratouy of Resource Environment and Sustainable Development of Oasis, Gansu Province, College of Geography and Environmental Science, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The obtained N, S-GQDs@PtNCs exhibited outstanding peroxidase activity, which could catalyze the oxidation of TMB by H2O2 and turned the solution blue. When Ag+ was added to the system, the Ag+ would be reduced to Ag0 by the capped N, S-GQDs and deposited on the surface of PtNCs, thereby significantly inhibiting the peroxidase activity of N, S-GQDs@PtNCs. The specific Ag-Pt interaction provides excellent selectivity toward Ag+ over other ions and Ag+ can interact with N, S-GQDs, which can facilitate electron transfer and accelerate the reaction rate.

In this work, a simple strategy for the rapid detection of Ag+ based on nitrogen, sulfur doped graphene quantum dots (N, S-GQDs) decorated platinum nanoclusters (PtNCs) was proposed for the first time. N, S-GQDs decorated PtNCs (N, S-GQDs@PtNCs) were prepared by a reduction method using ascorbic acid (AA) as reducing agent and N, S-GQDs as a stabilizer and morphology directing reagent, respectively. The N, S-GQDs@PtNCs have an excellent peroxidase activity, which could catalyze the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) by H2O2 to lead to an obvious color change. However, Ag+ could be reduced to Ag0 by N,S-GQDs, and the Ag0 covered the surface of N, S-GQDs@PtNCs causing its peroxidase activity to decrease. Ag+ concentration could be quantified by measuring the maximum absorption signal at 652 nm for oxidized TMB with a wide linear range of 0.5–300.0 nM and a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 0.2 nM. In addition, satisfactory results were obtained for the detection of Ag+ in actual water samples. The prepared colorimetric sensor had advantages such as simple operation, cost-effective, fast response, and visualization, and had great potential in detecting toxic Ag+ in environmental.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Silver is widely used in cosmetics and medical products because of its unique antibacterial properties [1]. However, excessive intake of Ag+ may lead to many serious diseases, including mitochondrial dysfunction, cytotoxicity and organ failure [2]. Because of the harmful effects of Ag+, the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) limits the maximum allowable content of Ag+ in drinking water to about 900 nM and Ag+ concentrations higher than 1.6 nM are toxic to fish and microorganisms [3]. Therefore, the sensitive detection of Ag+ in ecosystem is of great significance to environmental monitoring and public health. However, traditional analytical methods, such as atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [4], mass spectrometry [5], high performance liquid chromatography [6], inductively-coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [7], and electrochemical technology [8] require complex operation, time-consuming pretreatment, and relatively expensive instruments. For this reason, developing a cheap, simple, sensitive, and selective method for detecting Ag+ in environmental and biological samples is becoming increasingly important.

Since the peroxidase of Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles was first reported, nano-enzymes have attracted wide attention because of their low cost, high stability, biocompatibility, and controllable catalytic activity [9]. Up to now, people have been exploring the application prospect of nano-enzyme in medical diagnosis, environmental monitoring, food analysis, and so on [10]. Platinum-based nanomaterials have unique optical, electrical, and catalytic properties, and are one of the most important materials in the field of catalysis [11,12]. So far, people have devoted themselves to the study of platinum nanomaterials with controllable size and shape to obtain better utilization efficiency and catalytic performance, but the results are usually limited [13,14]. In order to improve the catalytic activity and ensure its stability, in recent years, some biomolecules, active groups, and flaky nanomaterials have played a key role in the synthesis or modification of platinum nanomaterials, such as citrate, bovine serum albumin, and oxidized graphene [15–19].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a kind of green zero-dimensional nanomaterials, which have aroused broad concern on account of their outstanding fluorescence characteristics, high light stability, good biocompatibility, and non-toxicity [20,21]. In addition, GQDs have been widely used as carrier materials due to their large specific surface area and abundant functional groups. Many efforts have demonstrated that when GQDs are doped with other elements (phosphorus (P), nitrogen (N), boron (B) or sulfur (S), etc.), the properties of GQDs will change. Heteroatoms can effectively modify the surface defects, and make their electronic density, optical properties, electrical properties, and chemical properties change accordingly [22–25]. Among them, N and S, the neighboring atoms with similar radius to carbon atoms in the periodic table of elements, are the most popular doping atoms in GQDs, which can effectively improve their fluorescence characteristics and provide useful functional groups for target sensing [26].

In this work, using N, S-GQDs as a stabilizer and morphology directing reagent, a simple chemical reduction method was used to synthesize N, S-GQDs@PtNCs with uniform size for the first time. The N, S-GQDs@PtNCs could catalyze the oxidation of TMB by H2O2 to generate blue product. After adding Ag+, the peroxidase activity of N, S-GQDs@PtNCs was significantly inhibited by the reduced Ag0.

The morphology, structure and properties of nanomaterials were analyzed by transmission electron microscope (TEM) (FEITECNAI20, USA) with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) (Gatan832). The surface composition and chemical state of the product were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) by SCALAB250XI electron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Quantitative analysis of absorbance at 652 nm by Ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (UV–vis) (China). N, S-GQDs was made in a Jing Hong GHG-9030A high temperature oven.

Chloroplatinic acid hexahydrate (H2PtCl6·6H2O), ascorbic acid (AA), citric acid (CA), thiourea acetic acid (HAc), sodium acetate anhydrous (NaAc), Silver nitrate (AgNO3), 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), thiourea, hydrogen peroxide 30 % (H2O2 30 %) and ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Other reagents were all of analytical grade and could be used without further purification. The secondary distilled water was used in the whole experiments.

Based on previous reports and slightly improved synthesis of N, S-GQDs, 1.68 g citric acid and 1.84 g thiourea were dissolved into 40 mL water, and stirred to form a clear solution at 55 °C for 3 h. Then the solution was transferred into 80 mL Teflon lined stainless autoclave, which was heated in an oven at 160 °C for 240 min [26]. After cooling down to room temperature, the solution was dialyzed for 2 days under a 3500 Da dialysis bag. Finally, the obtained solution was evaporated to 6 mL for standby.

Firstly, 0.31 mL of chloroplatinic acid (1 %), 10 mL water and 1 mL N, S-GQDs were added into a 50 mL round-bottom flask under stirring for 5 min at room temperature. Subsequently, AA (2.4 mL, 100 mM) was added dropwise into the mixture, which was heated at 80 °C for 30 min under reflux. When the solution was cooled to room temperature, it was centrifuged at 12000 rpm and washed twice with water.

For Ag+ sensing, N, S-GQDs@PtNCs (8 μL) and 40 µL different concentrations of Ag+ were added into the 1752 μL NaAc-HAc buffer (0.2 M, pH 4). Two minutes later, H2O2 (100 μL, 8 mM) and TMB (100 μL, 0.2 mM) were then added into the solution to initiate the chromogenic reaction and the reaction was continued for a further 10 min prior to spectroscopy measurement. Ultimately, the absorption spectrum obtained in the range of 500 ∼ 800 nm was recorded, and the highest absorption peak at 652 nm was used as the detection signal. The procedure of the specific experiment was the same except that Ag+ was replaced by other ions. EDTA as a masking agent to shield the interference of Hg2+.

The schematic diagram of Ag+ detection is shown in Scheme 1 . By using N, S-GQDs as a stabilizer and morphology directing reagent, chloroplatinic acid could be reduced by AA to generate stable and uniform PtNCs. The obtained N, S-GQDs@PtNCs exhibited outstanding peroxidase activity, which could catalyze the oxidation of TMB by H2O2 and turned the solution blue. When Ag+ was added to the system, the Ag+ would be reduced to Ag0 by the capped N, S-GQDs and deposited on the surface of PtNCs, thereby significantly inhibiting the peroxidase activity of N, S-GQDs@PtNCs. The specific Ag-Pt interaction provides excellent selectivity toward Ag+ over other ions and Ag+ can interact with N, S-GQDs, which can facilitate electron transfer and accelerate the reaction rate. The oxidation product of TMB has a maximum absorption peak at 652 nm, which can be used as the detection signal. On this basis, a colorimetric visual Ag+ sensor was developed by using N, S-GQDs@PtNCs to catalyze the colorimetric system of TMB and H2O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the formed N, S-GQDs@PtNCs
ce:section.ce:para: The size and morphology feature of N, S-GQDs@PtNCs were studied by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the TEM image of N, S-GQDs showed a uniform morphology with an average diameter of 2.17 ± 0.53 nm. The UV–vis spectra (Fig. S1) of N, S-GQDs showed three clear absorption bands at 234, 337, and 595 nm. The absorption peak at 234 nm was thanks to π->π* of CC, and the absorption at 337 nm corresponded to n->π* transition of the CO bond. The origin of these peaks was related to π electron transition in oxygen-containing GQDs. The absorption band at 595 nm was related to the doping of sulfur, which altered the surface state of GQDs. The band at 595 nm might be attributed to the π->π*, and n->π* of CS and SO [26]. As shown in Fig. 1B, the platinum nano materials were largely monodispersed and roughly cluster form and the average particle diameter was about 21.53 ± 0.68 nm. In addition, as shown in the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. S2), the continuous lattice spacing of 0.223 nm corresponding to the (111) facet of the face-centered cubic (fcc) Pt crystal. The N, S-GQDs@PtNCs elements obtained were confirmed by EDX, which indicated that the presence of N, S, and Pt elements (Fig. S3).

Compared with UV–vis spectrum (Fig. S4) of PtNPs without N, S-GQDs, the UV–vis spectrum (Fig. S1) of N, S-GQDs@PtNCs showed a broadband spectrum with a maximum absorption peak at 265 nm, which proved the N, S-GQDs were successfully wrapped around platinum. The measurement results of XPS further analyzed the chemical composition of the synthetic nanocomposite (Fig. S5). As shown in Fig. S5, the whole spectrum clearly exhibited that O, N, S, Pt, and C were present at the surface of PtNCs, indicating that platinum had been successfully restored. As shown in Fig. 2 , from the Pt 4f electron spectra, Gaussian-shaped spectrum (Pt 4f7/2 and Pt 4f5/2) with a separation of about 3.3 eV was emerged. The binding energies of Pt 4f7/2 70.4 and 74.7 eV could be attributed to Pt0 and Pt4+, respectively [3].

The peroxidase activity of the N, S-GQDs@PtNCs nanostructure was investigated by performing the catalytic reaction of TMB with H2O2. A series of control experiments were conducted in order to research the ability of the N, S-GQDs@PtNCs to catalyze the oxidation of TMB. As shown in Fig. S6, if only H2O2 and TMB or H2O2, TMB, and N, S-GQDs existed, no absorption signal was found, and there was no color change in the solution. Therefore N, S-GQDs catalytic activity was weak. If only TMB and N, S-GQDs@PtNCs existed, the absorption signal was very small and the color had hardly changed. However, when N, S-GQDs@PtNCs, TMB and H2O2 coexisted in the solution, the absorption signal at 652 nm increased significantly, and the solution color became dark blue. These experimental results showed that the N, S-GQDs@PtNCs had good peroxidase properties and could effectively catalyze the oxidation of TMB by H2O2.

In addition, to better understand the peroxidase activity of the N, S-GQDs@PtNCs, the steady-state dynamics of N, S-GQDs@PtNCs were measured (Fig. 3 A), and the kinetic parameters Km and Vmax were obtained using the Lineweaver-Burk equation (Eq. (1)) (Fig. 3B).
(1)
1 v
=
K m
V
max
[ S ]
+
1
V
max

The Michaelis constant (Km) is the substrate concentration when the reaction rate is half of the maximal velocity (Vmax), which can reflect the affinity of enzyme to the substrates. A lower Km indicates a higher affinity between material and TMB. The results showed that the Km value of N, S-GQDs@PtNCs based on TMB was lower than that of horse radish peroxidase (HRP) and other reported nanomaterials, which indicates that N, S-GQDs@PtNCs had higher affinity for TMB than other nano materials (Table S1).

In order to further explore the peroxidase activity mechanism of N, S-GQDs@PtNCs, the specific scavengers including •OH species scavenger (NaHCO3), O2 − scavenger superoxide dismutase (SOD), and 1O2 species scavenger (NaN3) were used to identify possible intermediates. As shown in Fig. S7, the latter two trapping agents could not inhibit the catalytic oxidation of N, S-GQDs@PtNCs, when NaHCO3 was added to the system, the absorption peak of TMBox (652 nm) dropped sharply, indicating that OH (it was produced by N, S-GQDs@PtNCs oxidizing H2O2) was the main active species for TMB oxidation. A visual colorimetric sensing system for N, S-GQDs@PtNCs based on these.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Ag+ on the catalytic activity of N, S-GQDs@PtNCs
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the feasibility study of Ag+ visualization detection strategy based on N, S-GQDs@PtNCs was carried out. In order to compare the catalytic performance of PtNPs synthesized by traditional reductant AA and PtNCs synthesized by adding N, S-GQDs, the catalytic performance was tested under the same conditions. Compare the c lines in Fig S8A and Fig S8B, N, S-GQDs@PtNCs had better catalytic activity. When different concentrations of Ag+ were added to the system, the color of the solution did not change significantly (Fig. S8A). N, S-GQDs@PtNCs could catalyze the oxidation of TMB by H2O2 to generate dark blue product with maximum absorbance at 652 nm. After the introduction of 50 nM Ag+, the catalytic activity of N, S-GQDs@PtNCs was inhibited to a certain extent, accompanied by fading of the TMB solution (Fig. S8B curve b). When the Ag+ concentration was increased to 200 nM, the absorption signal decreased significantly, indicating that Ag+ further inhibited the catalytic activity of N, S-GQDs@PtNCs (Fig. S8B curve a). By comparison, N, S-GQDs@PtNCs has a better affinity for Ag+. The decrease of the absorptivity of the system was related to the concentration of Ag+, which might be due to the formation of Pt-Ag metal bonds on the surface of N, S-GQDs@PtNCs. The added Ag+ interacted with Pt to form metallic bonds, and then Ag+ is reduced and deposited on the surface of N, S-GQDs@PtNCs.

In order to study the inhibition mechanism of the system, the XPS spectrum analysis was carried out on the after Ag+ treatment. It was used to measure whether Ag+ was reduced to Ag0 after interacting with N, S-GQDs@PtNCs. In addition, after being treated with Ag+, two distinct peaks appeared in the Ag 3d electronic spectra of N, S-GQDs@PtNCs (Fig. 4 ). The two signals Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2 appeared under the binding energy of 367.70 and 373.72 eV correspond to Ag0, which demonstrated the successful reduction of Ag+ on the surface of N, S-GQDs@PtNCs [9,27]. In theory, Pt has an inert noble metal character, so it cannot be oxidized by Ag+ under normal conditions. N, S-GQDs are a weak reducing agent, and it is difficult for Ag+ to react with N, S-GQDs at room temperature. However, N, S-GQDs can change the surface properties of PtNCs so that Ag+ can be reduced to Ag0 on its surface and inhibit the catalytic activity of the material.

A series of conditions of the reaction would be optimized in order to obtain the best sensing characteristics of the system. The type and pH of the reaction buffer had a great influence of TMB oxidation and the interaction. As shown in Fig. S9, the absorbance of this system is highest at pH 4, so it was selected to detect Ag+ at this pH. The absorption signals in the absence and presence of Ag+ were denoted by A0 and A, respectively (ΔA = A0 - A). As a catalyst, the concentration of N, S-GQDs@PtNCs would have a great impact on the system. As shown in Fig. 5 A, within a certain range, with the increase of its concentration, the absorption signal would increase, but when its concentration exceeded 2 μM, the detection sensitivity of Ag+ would decrease. According to the experimental results, the optimal concentration of material in the system is 2 μM, and it would be used in subsequent experiments. Next, H2O2, which played an important role in oxidizing TMB, was studied to explore the relationship between its concentration and the sensitivity. As shown in Fig. 5B, the ΔA value increased with the increase of H2O2 concentration, and tended to decrease when the H2O2 concentration exceeded 8 mM, so 8 mM was the optimal concentration of H2O2 in the system. The relationship between TMB concentration and system sensitivity was then explored. As shown in Fig. 5C, the ΔA value increased with the increase of TMB concentration, and tend to be stable when the TMB concentration exceeded 0.2 mM. Considering the economics of the experiment, so 0.2 mM was the optimal concentration of TMB in the system. Finally, the effect of the reaction time refers to Ag+ added into TMB-H2O2-N,S-GQDs@ PtNCs system was investigated. As shown in Fig. 5D, the ΔA of the N, S-GQDs@PtNCs was most significantly changed at 10 min, and the inhibition effect did not change significantly after that. Considering the efficiency of detection and the convenience of operation, 10 min was selected as the reaction time of the system. The optimum N, S-GQDs@PtNCs concentration, pH value, H2O2 concentration, TMB concentration and reduction time were 2 μM, 4, 8 mM, 0.2 mM, and 10 min, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of the Ag+ sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: Ag+ was detected under optimal conditions to estimate the sensitivity and range of colorimetric determination. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the absorption signal decreased with the increase of Ag+ concentration and was accompanied by fading of the solution. A large amount of Ag0 were reduced to the surface of N, S-GQDs@PtNCs, which inhibited the peroxidase activity and caused TMB to fail to produce blue oxidation products. Therefore, the color of the solution could be directly observed from dark blue to lighter. The results showed that there was a good linear correlation between Ag+ concentration and 652 nm absorption signal in the range of 0.5 ∼ 300 nM with a correlation coefficient of 0.998 (Fig. 6B). According to triplicate standard deviation over the blank response (3σ), the detection limit (LOD) of Ag+ was estimated to be 0.2 nM. Well below the maximum allowable level of Ag+ in drinking water set by the US Environmental Protection Agency (900 nM). The analytical ability of this sensor was compared with other Ag+ detection methods. As shown in Table 1 , the sensor had higher sensitivity than recently reported fluorescence, electrochemical and colorimetric methods. Such high sensitivity was due to the great catalytic ability of N, S-GQDs@PtNCs hybrids for TMB oxidation and the effective inhibition efficiency of N, S-GQDs reduced Ag0 for catalysis. In addition, the method was fast and economical due to the short Ag+ response time and no need for expensive peptides, DNA or fluorescent dyes. The high sensitivity and stability made N, S-GQDs@PtNCs have great potential for practical analytical applications of actual complex samples.

To explore the selectivity of the proposed sensor, interference studies were carried out for other metal ions present and some anions in the environment. Under the same conditions, the absorption of 300 nM Ag+ and other competing ions with a concentration of 20 times of Ag+ were tested. As shown in Fig. 7 , the absorption signal was significantly suppressed by Ag+, but the absorption signal of other ions (except Hg2+) was hardly disturbed. Since the ionic radius and reduction potential of Hg2+ and Ag+ are similar, Hg2+ could be adsorbed on N, S-GQDs@PtNCs and reduced. Although Hg2+ and Ag+ had the same experimental phenomenon under the condition of 20 times, we found that Hg2+ did not present a good linear relationship when we tried to detect Hg2+. Therefore the presence of Hg2+ could interfere with the detection, masking agents were required to eliminate the effect of Hg2+. EDTA was chosen as the masking agent. After it interacted with Hg2+, the influence of Hg2+ could be eliminated, which was because it formed a more stable complex with Hg2+ than Ag+ [3]. The above studies showed that N, S-GQDs@PtNCs could complete the specific detection of Ag+. What's more, the results of selectivity experiments could be judged by observing the change in color, so no particular instrument was required to distinguish the presence or absence of Ag+.

In order to study the practical application of the sensor for detecting Ag+, tap water and Yellow River water were used as actual samples for detection. Firstly, the collected Yellow River water was filtered through a 0.22 µm filter to remove insoluble matter. There was no detectable Ag+ existing in the tap water or Yellow River water samples. Therefore, the standard addition method was used, and different concentrations of Ag+ were added to the two water samples three times respectively, and then analyzed by the colorimetric sensing method. As shown in Table 2 , the recovery values ranging from 97.60 % to 104.6 % were gained, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was lower than 7 %. These results showed that the developed visual colorimetric sensor had good applicability in detecting the Ag+ of real samples.

In summary, a simple visual colorimetric sensor for rapid detection of Ag+ has been successfully developed with high selectivity and sensitivity. By effectively inhibiting the peroxidase activity of N, S-GQDs@PtNCs, the detection of Ag+ with high selectivity and sensitivity was achieved. The whole process could be completed in 12 min without any expensive instruments or complex operations. Compared with natural enzyme, the nano-composite material had the advantages of simple preparation, cost-effective, and high stability. Taking advantage of the high catalytic activity of N, S-GQDs@PtNCs, this method could detect Ag+ as low as 0.2 nM. In addition, the feasibility of this probe for detecting Ag+ in tap water and Yellow River water has been verified with satisfactory results. Therefore, this fabricated sensor can provide a novel portable, high-throughput visual sensing platform for Ag+ detection, and expand potential applications in environmental protection and clinical diagnosis by using quantum dots-based hybrid materials as high-performance catalysts.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial was sponsored by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21165016, 21175108, and 21265018), the Science and Technology Support Projects of Gansu Province (No.s 1011GKCA025, 090GKCA036, 1208RJZM289). the Postgraduate Research Funding Projects of Northwest Normal University (2021KYZZ02140).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.107827.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Oxidative stress-dependent toxicity of silver nanoparticles in human hepatoma cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Kim, J.E. Choi, J. Choi, K.H. Chung, K. Park, J. Yi, D.Y. Ryu, Oxidative stress-dependent toxicity of silver nanoparticles in human hepatoma cells, Toxicology in Vitro 23(6) (2009) 1076-1084.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A simple assay for ultrasensitive colorimetric detection of Ag+ at picomolar levels using platinum nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.W. Wang, M. Wang, L. Wang, H. Xu, S. Tang, H.H. Yang, L. Zhang, H. Song, A Simple Assay for Ultrasensitive Colorimetric Detection of Ag+ at Picomolar Levels Using Platinum Nanoparticles, Sensors (Basel) 17(11) (2017) 2521.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Silver chemical vapor generation for atomic absorption spectrometry: minimization of transport losses, interferences and application to water analysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Musil, J. Kratzer, M. Vobecký, O. Benada, T. Matoušek, Silver chemical vapor generation for atomic absorption spectrometry: minimization of transport losses, interferences and application to water analysis, Journal of Analytical Atomic Spectrometry 25(10) (2010) 1618-1626.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mass spectrometry techniques for imaging and detection of metallodrugs
